title: All-purpose surface spray
subtitle: A white vinegar and water spray with tea tree essential oil
excerpt: A multipurpose surface spray made from white vinegar, distilled water, and tea tree essential oil. Effective on kitchen surfaces, bathroom tiles, and glass. Not suitable for natural stone (marble, granite, limestone) where the acetic acid will etch the surface.
sourceNotes: Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), tea tree antimicrobial properties. UK Health Security Agency, Guidance on the use of household cleaning products for infection prevention and control (2023), acetic acid as a cleaning agent.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale to bottle size. 500 ml is the practical maximum for a trigger-spray bottle.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in an amber spray bottle away from direct sunlight. The acetic acid is self-preserving; the tea tree essential oil may fade in scent after 6-12 months but the cleaning effectiveness remains.

content.text: A white vinegar surface spray. White vinegar (5% acetic acid) is an effective surface disinfectant and degreaser; it cuts through grease and mineral deposits and inhibits bacteria and mould on cleaned surfaces. Tea tree essential oil adds a complementary antimicrobial action and masks the sharp vinegar smell. Distilled water prevents mineral deposits on the spray bottle valve and on glass surfaces.

Never mix this spray with bleach (chlorine gas), hydrogen peroxide (peracetic acid formation), or ammonia-based cleaners. The vinegar is acidic; do not use on natural stone surfaces (marble, granite, limestone, slate, terrazzo) where acetic acid will etch the surface over time. Safe on glass, sealed ceramic tiles, painted surfaces, stainless steel, and most plastics.

Tea tree essential oil is toxic to cats and dogs; if pets have access to surfaces sprayed with this product, use an unscented version (white vinegar and water only) or substitute a pet-safe essential oil such as lavender at 0.5%.

items.prepNote: 5% acetic acid white vinegar. Standard household cleaning vinegar. 50% of the spray.
distilled or deionised water. 50% minus the EO volume. Tap water leaves mineral deposits on glass; distilled avoids this.

Melaleuca alternifolia tea tree essential oil. 0.8% of the total volume (approximately 20 drops). Provides antimicrobial action and masks the vinegar scent. Shake the bottle before each use as the oil and water separate on standing.

content.text: Pour the white vinegar into the amber spray bottle. Add the distilled water. Add the tea tree essential oil. Fit the trigger-spray top and shake well for 30 seconds to emulsify the oil as much as possible (it will still separate on standing; shake before each use). Label the bottle with the contents and the batch date.

Shake the bottle. Spray onto the surface and wipe with a clean cloth or paper towel. For greasy surfaces, spray and leave for 30 seconds before wiping. For bathroom mineral deposits, spray and leave for 5 minutes before wiping. The vinegar smell dissipates as it dries.

Safe on: sealed ceramic tiles, glass, stainless steel, painted walls (test in an inconspicuous area first), most hard plastics. Not safe on: natural stone (marble, granite, slate, limestone, terrazzo), cast iron, waxed wood, and aluminium (vinegar pits aluminium over time). Use plain water or a dedicated stone cleaner on stone surfaces.

12 months in an amber spray bottle. The acetic acid is self-preserving; the spray does not go off. The tea tree essential oil scent fades over 6-12 months but the cleaning effectiveness remains. Keep away from direct sunlight, which degrades the essential oil faster.
